turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the Court in Case 15-214, Murr versus Wisconsin.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: The Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment provides that private property shall not be taken for public use without just compensation and that clause is applicable to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. This Court has explained that a regulation that goes too far constitutes a taking. Today's case presents a preliminary question for the regulatory takings analysis. What is the proper unit of property against which to assess the effect of a challenged governmental regulation? Petitioners here are the Murr brothers and sisters. They own two adjacent lots along the lower portion of the St. Croix River in Wisconsin. The lots are called Lot E and Lot F. For the areas where petitioners' property is located, state and local regulations prevent the sale or use of adjacent lots under common ownership as separate building sites unless they meet a size requirement. Petitioners' parents purchased Lot E and Lot F separately in the 1960s and they maintained them under separate ownership. They then transferred Lot F to the petitioners in 1994 and Lot E to the petitioners in 1995, so they are under the same ownership. Lot E and Lot F do not, by themselves, separately meet the size requirement necessary to sell or develop them separately. To avoid the effect of the state and local rules, petitioners sought variances from the local Board of Adjustment. The Board denied the variance, the Wisconsin courts affirmed. The State Court of Appeals found that the local ordinance effectively merged the two lots. This means that petitioners can sell or build only on the single combined lot. Petitioners filed a suit. They alleged that the restrictions worked a regulatory taking that deprived them of all or practically all of the use of Lot E. The trial court granted summary judgment to the State of Wisconsin, the State Court of Appeals affirmed. The background of this Court's Takings Clause jurisprudence informs the Court's approach today. The jurisprudence is marked by its flexibility which is a means to reconcile two competing objectives, the individual's right to retain the interest of the core of private property ownership and the government's power to adjust rights for the common good. The Court today holds that a number of objective factors must be considered in determining what property becomes the denominator of the takings inquiry, one lot or both lots together. The inquiry should determine whether reasonable expectations about property ownership would lead a land owner to anticipate that his holdings would be treated as one parcel or separate tracks. First, the court should give substantial weight to the property's treatment under state and local law, in particular, how it is bounded and divided. Second, the court should consider the property's physical characteristics including the physical relationship of any distinguishable tracks, topography and the surrounding human and ecological environment. Third, the court should assess the property's value under the challenged regulation with special attention to the effect of the burdened land on the value of other holdings. Under the appropriate multifactor standard, petitioners' property should be evaluated as a single parcel consisting of Lots E and F together. The valid merger of the lots under state law informs the reasonable expectation that the lots will be treated as a single property. As to the property's physical characteristics, the lots are contiguous, their terrain and shape make it reasonable to expect that their range of potential uses might be limited. Petitioners moreover could have anticipated regulation of the property due to its location along the river which are regulated by federal, state and local law long before the petitioners acquired the land. Finally, Lot E brings perspective value to Lot F. The restriction on using the individual lots is mitigated by the benefits of using the property as an integrated whole. The Court of Appeals was thus correct to treat the contiguous properties as one parcel and furthermore correct to conclude that petitioners cannot establish a compensable taking. The judgment of the Court of Appeals of Wisconsin is affirmed. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Thomas and Justice Alito joined. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
